output:
Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid where each column is uniform top-to-bottom, and the three columns use three different colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 3x3 size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recolor each column by shifting its color according to the column position: the left, middle, and right columns are mapped to the next three colors in a fixed ordered palette, specifically 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, 4→3, 8→9, 6→2, 9→8, 5→1, while preserving the column structure.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a checker-like mosaic built from 4x4 blocks of colors, with occasional missing cells shown as black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the underlying 4x4 template from the intact portions of the image, then tile that template across the entire grid so that all missing black positions are restored to their corresponding template colors.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a repeated 4x4 tile pattern, with one or more 4x4 regions altered by black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recover the original full repeating pattern by filling every black cell with the color that would appear there in the unbroken periodic tile, using the non-black parts of the grid to infer the repeating 4x4 motif.

Describing the input grid: The input is made of a large grid whose rows and columns repeat with period 4, except that some cells are erased to black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reconstruct the complete periodic grid by copying each row pattern every 4 rows and each column pattern every 4 columns, replacing all black cells with the value determined by that 4-step periodicity.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 colored patterns of the same color, one above and one below a horizontal separator region of black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored pattern, extend every row of the pattern horizontally from its leftmost colored cell to its rightmost colored cell, keeping the original colored rows and leaving all other rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid where each of the four nonzero rows represents a pair of endpoint colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero row, if the two endpoint colors are the same, fill the entire row with that color; if the two endpoint colors are different, leave the row unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains cyan cells (8) that act as anchors in an empty grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every row or column that contains two or more cyan anchors, replace the cells between consecutive anchors with green (3), producing a straight horizontal or vertical green segment connecting the anchors. Keep all cyan anchor cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains cyan endpoints (8) placed on rows or columns, sometimes with more than two endpoints aligned.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: In each row and each column, find the leftmost/rightmost or topmost/bottommost cyan markers. If there are exactly two markers aligned, draw a green line between them by coloring all intervening black cells. If there are more than two aligned markers, only fill the segment between the outermost pair.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more cyan marker cells (8) on an otherwise black grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each pair of cyan markers in the same row or column, fill every black cell strictly between them with green (3). Leave the cyan marker cells unchanged and do not alter any other cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid containing a colored pattern near the top, middle, or upper-left area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Translate all nonzero cells downward by exactly one row without changing their columns, preserving their colors, and fill the vacated top row with zeros. Cells in the bottom row are removed if shifted out of bounds.

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid containing one colored 1-cell-thick horizontal or slanted pattern made of nonzero cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Shift every nonzero cell one row downward, keeping the same column, and replace the top row with zeros. If a cell would move beyond the bottom edge, discard it.

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid with a nonzero pattern occupying some cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Move the entire nonzero pattern down by one row, preserving relative positions and colors, and set the top row to all zeros. Any cells shifted past the bottom edge are omitted.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a single colored column on the left with blank space to the right.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid size matches the input grid size exactly.
Describing how to transform the grid: Preserve the leftmost column. In the remaining cells, draw a red anti-diagonal whose length equals the number of rows minus one, starting from the top-right corner and ending one row above the bottom-left corner. Set every cell in the last row to yellow.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with one nonzero-colored first column and the rest of the cells empty, except that the first column color identifies the object.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same number of rows and columns as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the first column unchanged. Fill the bottom row with yellow. Place red cells on the anti-diagonal of the remaining rows, starting at the top-right corner and moving one step down-left each row, while leaving all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: In the input, there are several 3x3 blocks of a single color arranged in different positions, with one grey cell acting as a selector.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the 3x3 block whose colored cells are in the same connected component as the grey cell’s nearest colored cell, then output that block normalized so its colored cells are shifted into a 3x3 canvas and recolored to the block color, preserving the exact black/non-black layout.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of colored rectangular regions whose border cells define the shape, with black background separating them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each rectangle, identify its interior by eroding the rectangle by one cell from all sides. Then fill that eroded interior with teal (8). Rectangles with no interior after erosion remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains multiple axis-aligned rectangles of different sizes and colors, each isolated by black space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each rectangle, determine whether it is at least 3 cells tall and 3 cells wide. If so, replace all cells strictly inside its border with teal (8); if the rectangle is only 1 or 2 cells thick in either dimension, leave it unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several separate colored rectangular frames or filled rectangles, each made of one nonzero color on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each connected nonzero rectangle, find its interior cells (cells not on the border of the rectangle). Recolor every interior cell to teal (8), while leaving the border cells and all black cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty square grid containing one colored cell that indicates an anchor position.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the anchor color into every cell that lies on either of the two diagonals passing through the anchor, and then extend the same color outward by reflecting the diagonal placement across the center of the grid, producing a full X-like arrangement of colored cells on a black background.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty square grid with one or more nonzero cells, usually a single colored cell acting as a seed.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the nonzero seed cell and fill the entire grid with a diagonal mirror pattern centered on that cell: every cell at the same Chebyshev distance from the seed along the two diagonals receives the seed color, creating an X-shaped symmetry pattern. If there are multiple seeds, use the unique nonzero cell of the input as the center of the generated pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid with a single colored pixel placed somewhere off-center.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the colored pixel as a reference point and draw the set of all cells whose row and column offsets from the reference have equal absolute value, i.e. cells satisfying |r-r0| = |c-c0|, with the reference color, leaving all other cells black. This creates the diagonal cross pattern seen in the outputs.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square 3x3 grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x6.
Describing how to transform the grid: Form the output by tiling four transformed versions of the input: top-left is the original, top-right is rotated 90 degrees clockwise, bottom-left is rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise, and bottom-right is rotated 180 degrees. The four quadrants are assembled in that order into a 2x2 block.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of one or more isolated 2x2 grey blocks arranged on an empty grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each grey 2x2 block as the center of a 4x4 stencil. In the cells immediately adjacent diagonally to the block, draw a 1-cell colored corner pattern: blue at the northwest corner, red at the northeast corner, green at the southwest corner, and yellow at the southeast corner. The pattern is placed relative to the block’s top-left cell, so the colored cells appear one row above/left, above/right, below/left, and below/right of the block.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more 2x2 grey blocks placed on a large black canvas.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey 2x2 block, place four 1-cell markers around it at the four diagonal corner positions of the 4x4 region centered on that block: top-left gets blue (1), top-right gets red (2), bottom-left gets green (3), and bottom-right gets yellow (4). Leave the original grey block unchanged and do this for every grey block independently.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several separated 2x2 grey squares on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 2x2 grey square, extend it by one cell in every diagonal direction to form a 4x4 pattern centered on the square. Fill the four diagonal corner cells of that 4x4 area with colors 1, 2, 3, and 4 in reading order around the square: upper-left = 1, upper-right = 2, lower-left = 3, lower-right = 4. If multiple expanded areas do not overlap, apply this to all grey squares.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 grids separated by a full row of 4s, with the top grid in blue/black and the bottom grid in red/black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output by marking 3 in every cell where exactly one of the two input grids has a nonzero value, and 0 where both grids are either nonzero or both zero.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more colored seed cells placed in an otherwise black grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored seed cell, draw an L-shape anchored at that seed: fill all cells to the right in the same row from the seed to the last column, and fill all cells below in the same column from the seed to the last row. All filled cells use the seed’s color, and black cells remain unchanged unless covered by one of these L-shapes.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with a few isolated nonzero “seed” cells of different colors, and all other cells are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero seed cell at position (r, c), extend that cell’s color horizontally to the right until the end of its row, and also extend it vertically downward from the last column of the grid in the same color. More precisely, color every cell in the same row with columns c through the last column, and color every cell in the same column below the seed only if it lies in the rightmost column; if multiple seeds affect a cell, the later/lower seed is not needed because the examples have non-overlapping effects.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a background of 1s with one or more non-1 colored blocks arranged in a rectangular area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size equals the height and width of the rectangular area occupied by the non-1 cells.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop to the bounding rectangle of all non-1 cells, then convert every background cell that lies inside that crop from 1 to 0, preserving the positions and colors of the non-1 cells.

Describing the input grid: The input has a uniform 1-colored frame/background and a compact pattern of other colors embedded within it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the minimal rectangle spanning all cells that are not 1.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find all cells whose value is not 1, take their bounding box, and output that subgrid with all 1s changed to 0 so that only the embedded pattern remains against a black background.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large background of 1s containing a smaller non-1 rectangular region made of other colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size is the bounding box of the non-1 region.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the smallest rectangle that contains all cells whose value is not 1, and then replace every 1 inside that extracted rectangle with 0 while keeping the other colors unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more hollow 5-colored rectangles, each acting as a container around an empty interior.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect each hollow rectangle and fill its interior with a color chosen by the rectangle’s interior size: interiors of size 1 are colored fuchsia (6), interiors of size 2x2 are colored orange (7), and interiors of size 3x3 are colored teal (8). The border cells remain 5.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains only the colors 1, 7, and 8, with 7 appearing as the color to be modified.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every cell whose value is 7 with 5, and leave every other cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a set of vertical stripes or blocks where each colored cell type is paired with background cells and often overlaid on color 5 regions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, identify the leftmost non-5 nonzero color present in that row and repaint all nonzero cells in that row with that color, leaving zeros unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing several isolated grey cells placed in some rows and columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each grey cell with a 3x3 block of blue cells whose top-left corner is aligned to the grey cell’s position rounded down to the nearest multiple of 3 in both row and column. All other cells remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid with grey cells marking the centers of intended filled squares.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every grey cell, draw a 3x3 blue square centered on that cell. If multiple 3x3 squares overlap, the overlapping cells stay blue. All non-covered cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid with grey marker cells that indicate where 3x3 regions should be filled.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Partition the grid into nine 3x3 subgrids. For each subgrid, if it contains at least one grey cell in the input, color the entire subgrid blue in the output; otherwise leave that subgrid black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x10 canvas with a few single colored marker cells in the top row, a blank separator row, and then several 2x2 or 3x4 rectangular blocks made of one repeated nonzero color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each connected nonzero rectangular block below the separator, replace every cell of that block with the color of the nearest marker cell in the top row when measured by column alignment: blocks in the left half take the left marker color, blocks in the middle take the middle marker color, and blocks in the right half take the right marker color; the top marker row and separator row remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x11 panels separated by a full horizontal divider row of color 5. The top panel uses one nonzero color, and the bottom panel uses another nonzero color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has height 5 and width 11.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output by taking the top 5x11 panel as a template and the bottom 5x11 panel as a second template. For each cell, if exactly one of the two panels has a nonzero value at that position, place that nonzero value in the output; if both are zero, keep zero. If both panels are nonzero at the same position, use the bottom panel’s color.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x11 patterns separated by a row of 5s. The top pattern and bottom pattern are complementary masks over the same 5x11 area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has size 5x11.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the bottom pattern onto the top pattern by shifting the bottom pattern upward by 5 rows and combining them cellwise. In the combined result, preserve all colored cells from both halves, but when a cell is colored in both halves, keep the color from the lower half.

Describing the input grid: The input is two stacked 5x11 grids with a separator row of 5s. Each grid contains sparse colored cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 5x11.
Describing how to transform the grid: Form the output by taking the union of the nonzero cells from the top and bottom grids after aligning them by column and row within their 5x11 regions. If a cell is nonzero in only one region, copy that value; if it is nonzero in both, copy the value from the lower region.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing one background color and one foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 3x3 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the grid across the anti-diagonal, mapping cell (r, c) to (2-c, 2-r).

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing one background color and one foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 3x3 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Rotate the entire grid 90 degrees counterclockwise.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single polyomino of 4s on a 9x9 black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size is determined by the span of the object after applying a fixed 2x2 replication.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace each 4-cell in the input with a 2x2 block of 4s, preserving the input's relative arrangement. Then crop away all-black rows and columns from the result to produce the output grid.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one connected 4-shape that can be decomposed into horizontal and vertical runs.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a compact rectangle whose dimensions depend on the longest horizontal and vertical extents of the shape.
Describing how to transform the grid: Convert each occupied cell into a 2x2 square, but only for the cells that belong to the shape's outer boundary; interior-adjacent cells are not duplicated. After this boundary-based upscaling, crop the smallest rectangle containing all 4s.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid made of repeated 2x2 colored blocks separated by rows and columns of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x1 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each non-zero color, count the total number of times it appears in the 2x2 blocks. Then take the three colors with the smallest positive counts, ordered from most frequent to least frequent among those three, and write them top to bottom in the output.
